coredata.dc:title: Paper-based printed electrochemical sensor for the detection of hydroxymethyl furaldehyde in foods using nitrogen and sulphur co-doped graphene quantum dots
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Saisree, S.
Aleena, C.J.
Chandran, Achu
coredata.dc:description: In this study, we have developed a paper-based screen-printed electrode (SPE) system for the detection of 5-hydroxymethylfurfural (HMF), a potential carcinogen commonly found in food products, which is formed during the acidic decomposition of monosaccharides. The SPE was modified with nitrogen and sulphur co-doped graphene quantum dots (N,S-GQDs), synthesized using a simple hydrothermal process with amino acid precursors, tryptophan and methionine to achieve an innovative doping of nitrogen and sulphur respectively within the aromatic skeleton of the GQDs. The resulting N,S-GQDs exhibited excellent sensitivity (0.756 μAμM−1 cm−2) and selectivity for HMF detection through the paper-based SPE, which is a cost-effective, portable, and real-time sensing platform. In addition, the developed N,S-GQD/SPE sensor showed a ∼ 2.5-fold higher current response than the conventional glassy carbon electrode (GCE) system. The developed SPE sensor demonstrated a limit of detection (LOD) of 1 μM which is 10 times better than GCE, along with a broad linear detection range. Furthermore, the N,S-GQD/SPE effectively detected HMF in real samples, achieving recovery rates of approximately 99 % across all tested matrices. These results highlight the potential of the proposed system for real-time monitoring of HMF in food, paving the way for its application in food safety.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Paper-based sensor
Screen-printed sensor
Hydroxymethyl furaldehyde
Nitrogen and sulphur co-doped graphene quantum dots
Electrochemical sensing

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis, structure, size, and properties of N,S-GQD
EC sensing studies and the mechanism of HMF sensing

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The Director, CSIR-NIIST, is gratefully acknowledged for his continuous support and encouragement in this work. The Author Saisree acknowledges the funding and support from the Kerala State Higher Education Council, Govt. of Kerala, through the Prestigious NavaKerala PDF (CM's NKPDF, G.O. (Ms) no. 594/2022/HEDN and G.O. (Rt) no. 606/2023/HEDN).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A and B) The HR-TEM micrographs of N,S-GQD and the equivalent d-spacing values; (C) the histogram corresponding to the TEM images of N,S-GQD; (D) the absorption spectrum of N,S-GQD; (E) the emission profile of N,S-GQD; (F) the digital images showing the photoluminescence of the N,S-GQD under visible (a), (c) and 365 nm short-UV (b), (d) radiation of solution and solid-state, respectively.

(A) and (B) The CV and DPV profiles of HMF in 0.1 M NaOH on N,S-GQD/GCE for the range of concentrations 10−6 to 10−3 M, respectively; (C) and (D) The CV and DPV curves of HMF that displays peak initiating at 10−5 M confirms the LOD, respectively; (E)The equivalent linear dynamic ranges gained for the range of concentrations 10−5 to 10−3 M HMF.

(A) and (B) The DPV responses of 1 mM of HMF in 0.1 M NaOH on N,S-GQD/SPE and N,S-GQD/GCE, respectively; (C) a comparative bar diagram showing the enhanced current response on N,S-GQD/SPE over N,S-GQD/GCE; (D) the DPV profile of HMF that displays peak initial at 10−11 M confirms the LOD on N,S-GQD/SPE.
Graphic of detailed synthesis and an illustrative N,S-GQD structure.
The schematic diagram showing the EC sensing of HMF by N,S-GQD modified SPE.
Comparison of the LOD and LDR of N,S-GQD/SPE with that given in the literature reports of various EC HMF sensors.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The recovery outcomes of HMF in real sample studies.

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The recovery amounts come within the RSD of ∼0.7 % in every sample tested.

head.ce:title: Paper-based printed electrochemical sensor for the detection of hydroxymethyl furaldehyde in foods using nitrogen and sulphur co-doped graphene quantum dots

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Materials Science and Technology Division, CSIR-National Institute of Interdisciplinary Science and Technology, Industrial Estate, Thiruvananthapuram 695 019, Kerala, India.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: CSIR-National Institute of Interdisciplinary Science and Technology, Industrial Estate

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, we have developed a paper-based screen-printed electrode (SPE) system for the detection of 5-hydroxymethylfurfural (HMF), a potential carcinogen commonly found in food products, which is formed during the acidic decomposition of monosaccharides. The SPE was modified with nitrogen and sulphur co-doped graphene quantum dots (N,S-GQDs), synthesized using a simple hydrothermal process with amino acid precursors, tryptophan and methionine to achieve an innovative doping of nitrogen and sulphur respectively within the aromatic skeleton of the GQDs. The resulting N,S-GQDs exhibited excellent sensitivity (0.756 μAμM−1 cm−2) and selectivity for HMF detection through the paper-based SPE, which is a cost-effective, portable, and real-time sensing platform. In addition, the developed N,S-GQD/SPE sensor showed a ∼ 2.5-fold higher current response than the conventional glassy carbon electrode (GCE) system. The developed SPE sensor demonstrated a limit of detection (LOD) of 1 μM which is 10 times better than GCE, along with a broad linear detection range. Furthermore, the N,S-GQD/SPE effectively detected HMF in real samples, achieving recovery rates of approximately 99 % across all tested matrices. These results highlight the potential of the proposed system for real-time monitoring of HMF in food, paving the way for its application in food safety.

ce:keyword.ce:text: Nitrogen and sulphur co-doped graphene quantum dots

ce:section.ce:para: Food is one of the most fundamental necessities for all living beings, and consuming high-quality food is essential for maintaining good health. However, the quality of many foods we consume daily is compromised due to processing and adulteration. Food processing involves any modification of its natural state through cooking, seasoning, preservation, or combination with other ingredients. During these ultra processing some carcinogenic compounds are getting formed in food stuffs. Approximately one-third of cancer cases are linked to diet [1], necessitating the identification of carcinogenic substances in food. Mitigating risk factors and enhancing prevention, detection, and treatment strategies are crucial to combating this growing threat. This underscores the critical need for food safety and awareness of cooking related risks [2]. The prominent food items that are associated with enhanced cancer rates include genetically modified foods, grilled red meat, salted, pickled, and smoked foods, hydrogenated oils, soda and carbonated beverages, farmed fish, white flour, microwave popcorn, canned foods, and refined sugar [3–6].

5-Hydroxymethyl-2-furaldehyde (HMF), also known as Hydroxymethyl furfural, is a carcinogenic substance primarily found in monosaccharide-rich foods such as fruit juices, brown sugar, fruit jams, honey, milk, grain-based products, and bread [7–11]. HMF is a cyclic aldehyde formed through the acidic decomposition of monosaccharides. It naturally develops in products where monosaccharides are exposed to an acidic environment in the presence of water, particularly during extended storage under such conditions [12]. HMF is also produced during pasteurization and cooking due to sugar dehydration and is an early-stage byproduct of the Maillard reaction, influencing food color and flavor [8]. Additionally, HMF can be generated through the condensation of carbohydrates with free amine groups. HMF is known for its genotoxic properties, capable of inducing DNA strand breaks and chromosomal aberrations [12]. It has also been linked to nephrotoxicity, immunotoxicity, and carcinogenicity [8]. Given the significant health risks associated with HMF, it is crucial to establish a well-structured and effective detection system. Furthermore, its presence serves as a key indicator of sugar degradation, making it an essential parameter in food quality control.

In recent years, various analytical techniques have been developed for the detection of HMF. These methods include: Fluorescence Spectroscopic Detection [7], Liquid Chromatographic Detection-Ion Exchange Liquid Chromatography [8], Automated Flow Injection Analysis (FIA) [9], High Performance Liquid Chromatography [10], and Near-Infrared Spectroscopy [11]. However, conventional methods are often labour-intensive and require specialized expertise, limiting their practical application in real-world scenario [12]. Here comes the potentiality of electrochemical (EC) sensors [13] with its inherently fast, accurate, compact, simple, portable, and cost-effective properties. [14]. EC sensors are among the most prevalent types of chemical sensors, where the reactions occur between the analytes and the sensor, generating electrical signals proportional to the analyte concentration [15]. The potential applicability [16] of EC sensors in the areas of agriculture, food, environmental, and biomedical sectors are well understood from the intensive research works going on in these areas [17].

The integration of novel materials into electrochemical (EC) sensing systems is driven by continuous innovation to meet the evolving demands of modern EC sensor technology. Among carbon-based nanomaterials, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have gained significant attention due to their remarkable EC activity, attributed to their active edge sites, biocompatibility, solubility, and ease of synthesis [18]. Owing to quantum confinement and edge effects, GQDs exhibit rapid electron transport and exceptional conductivity, along with notable optical properties [19]. These characteristics make GQDs ideal candidates for facilitating high-speed electron transfer, enhancing electrocatalytic charge transfer reactions, and improving the signal-to-noise ratio in sensors [20]. Furthermore, heteroatom doping can further enhance these properties by introducing surface-active sites, non-bonded electrons, and extended conjugation [18].

The hetero atom doping can be achieved by adding doping agents or rather choosing a precursor having the potential of in situ hetero atom doping. Here in this work we adopted an in situ hetero atom doping strategy towards two hetero atoms, nitrogen (N) and sulphur (S). We particularly chosen the amino acids (AA); tryptophan (TRY) and methionine (MET), as the precursors. Both the AAs serve as N sources, with MET also capable of act as an effective S source to introduce sulphur functionalities in to the GQD structure [21]. The combination is reporting for the first time, and the GQD formed is termed as nitrogen and sulphur-doped graphene quantum dot (N,S-GQD). The selection of TRY as a nitrogen source is particularly advantageous due to the presence of a nitrogen atom within its aromatic indole ring system, which can be effectively incorporated into the aromatic basal planes of the resulting GQD structure. We have followed a much simpler and widely employed hydrothermal synthesis as a cost-effective, non-toxic, and environmentally friendly strategy for GQD production [22]. N,S-GQD with N and S doping can increase the active sites, further improving conductivity and electrocatalytic activity, contributes to enhanced performance in EC sensing.

In the present study, the N,S-GQD modified glassy carbon electrode (N,S-GQD/GCE) demonstrated exceptional sensitivity towards HMF, achieving a limit of detection (LOD) of 10 μM (10−5 M). But, in order to avail the real-time applicability, the conventional bulkier electrochemical system has to be miniaturised [23]. For that, we have transferred the GCE technology to a screen-printed sensor with working (WE), counter (CE) and reference electrodes (RE) were printed on a paper substrate (SPE). The paper-based sensor can further reduce the cost of the system. The N,S-GQD modified SPE were then tested for the real-time applicability. As expected, the current response and the LOD values got improved on SPE, because of replacing the GCE with a more conductive carbon ink, as well as the reduced proximity between the WE, RE, and CE within the SPE [23], compare to the conventional bulkier system. The current response on N,S-GQD/SPE got enhanced by ∼2.5 -fold with a 10-times lower LOD, 1 μM. Thus, because of the cost-effectiveness, easiness to synthesize, and the excellent electrochemical properties with excellent selectivity, N,S-GQD-based sensor allows for the sensitive HMF detection, making it ideal for food safety, quality control, and other applications without requiring complicated instrumentations or training. Further, the successful integration of the sensor onto an SPE signifies a foremost advancement on the way to the development of a commercially feasible device with enhanced sensing abilities, moving faster to practical applications in real-time monitoring.

MET, TRY, Glucose (GLU), Ascorbic acid (AA), Sodium hydroxide (NaOH), KCl, CaCO3, MgCO3, Acrylamide (ACR) were procured from Sigma-Aldrich Pvt. 2-methyl furan (MF), HMF, 2-ethyl furan (EF), 2,5-dimethyl furan (DMF) and acrolein (ACN) were purchased from Tokyo Chemical Industry (India) Pvt. Ltd. All chemicals acquired were of analytical grade and were used without any purifications further. Deionised water was utilised in every procedure if not stated or else.

JEOL JEM 2100 electron microscope with 200 kV accelerating voltage was utilised for high-resolution transmission electron microscopic (HR-TEM) studies. PerkinElmer spectrum100 Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy was used for recording the IR spectral data in universal attenuated total reflection (UATR) mode, Renishaw inVia Raman microscope was utilised for Raman spectroscopy measurements with 532 nm excitation wavelength. CARY 100 Bio UV–Visible spectrophotometer were used to get the absorption spectra data. Horiba FluoroMax-4 spectrometer gave the photoluminescence spectra, with an excitation and emission slits of 1 and 2 nm, respectively. X-Ray photoelectron spectroscopic analyses were done in PHI 5000 Versa Probe II spectrophotometer (ULVAC-PHI Inc., USA), equipped with a micro-focused (200 mm, 15 kV) Al-Ka monochromatic 1486.6 eV X- Ray source. Bruker D8 discovery small angle X-ray diffractometer (XRD) gave the X-ray diffractograms in the excitation source: λ = 1.54 Å, Cu Kα radiation.

The N,S-GQD was synthesized by a hydrothermal process utilising the amino acids, MET) and TRY as precursors. The precursors MET and TRY were taken in 1:1 ratio. To the reaction mixture, a few drops of NaOH is added and is ultrasonicated for 30 min. The reaction mixture is then transferred to a Teflon lined autoclave. It is then kept at 220 °C for 12 h to get the N,S-GQD. Afterwards, the solution was allowed to reach the room temperature naturally and later the supernatant liquid including N,S-GQD was collected.

The glassy carbon working electrode (GCE), were mechanically refined with a moisturized micro cloth with powder alumina (0.05 mm micro polish powder from CH instruments). The electrodes were then washed vigilantly for 2 min with distilled water by ultrasonication. After every single analysis, GCE were prepared as defined just above. The materials were dro-cast over the prepared GCE with utmost care and permitted to dehydrate at room temperature for 24 h to attain the modified GCE.

The screen-printed electrodes (SPE), were fabricated by means of a semiautomatic screen printer (XPRT2, Ekra, Bo¨nnigheim, Germany) following the steps, that is already reported from our group [16]. In brief, appropriate ink materials corresponding to working electrode (WE), counter electrode (CE) and reference electrodes (RE) were screen-printed on a paper substrate. Printing three contact pads in a straight line is the initiation step in printing of the SPE, and for that silver ink (Metalon 03LV, Nova Centrix, Austin, USA) purchased commercially was utilised. It was then followed by black carbon ink (Metalon 03LV, Nova Centrix, Austin, USA) as WE and CE. Further, the Ag/AgCl ink (Sigma-Aldrich, India), purchased commercially, was in print on the RE area. Last of all, a dielectric grey (902,497, Sigma-Aldrich, India) was in print on the SPE by covering the portions on the substrate selectively.

The electrochemical performance of the analytes (GLU, AA, KCl, CaCO3, MgCO3, ACR, HMF, EF, MF, DMF and ACN) on the surface of the N,S-GQD/GCE and N,S-GQD/SPE were examined by means of cyclic voltammetry (CV), and differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) studies at room temperature. The potentiostat/galvanostat CHI660F, CH Instruments system was used for all the electrochemical measurements. The measurements were carried out in combination with the three-electrode system entailing, a modified 3 mm diameter glassy carbon working electrode, a platinum wire counter electrode and an Ag/AgCl (saturated KCl) reference electrode on an all-printed SPE on a paper substrate as well. 0.1 M NaOH was used as the supporting electrolyte, if not specified then.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis, structure, size, and properties of N,S-GQD
ce:section.ce:para: The N,S-GQD was synthesized following the protocol outlined in the Experimental section using MET, and TRY. In this synthesis, the amino acids are taken as the carbon source for GQD synthesis, since amino acids can serve as an excellent dual-purpose precursor for the synthesis of GQD. Their carbon backbone provides the required carbon source for the graphitic structure, while the amine (−NH2) functionality can act as N doping agents [21]. Also, the S present in MET can contribute towards S co-doping into the aromatic skeleton as well. The heteroatom doping in GQDs is known to enhance their optical, electronic, and chemical properties by introducing additional active sites and modifying the electronic band structure [18]. Thus, amino acids make a versatile and eco-friendly choice for producing heteroatom functionalized GQD. The as obtained GQD is thus termed as N,S-GQD. A schematic elucidation of the procedure of N,S-GQD synthesis is depicted in detail in Scheme 1 .

The HR-TEM of the obtained N,S-GQD are presented as Fig. 1 A. The lower-magnification image provides an overview of the N,S-GQD, revealing details about their size distribution and uniformity. The Fig. 1B shows lattice fringes of crystallinity with 0.21 nm interlayer spacing, corresponding to the diffraction planes (102) of sp2 graphitic carbon, confirms the effective GQD formation [20]. The histogram (Fig. 1C) obtained corresponding to Fig. 1, indicate the typical size distribution of N,S-GQD is between ∼4 to 4.5 nm.

The absorption spectrum (Fig. 1D) exhibit two distinct absorption bands at ∼288 nm, and ∼ 345 nm. The first band attributed to the π - π* transition of aromatic CC and the latter corresponding to the n - π* transition involving the non-bonded electrons of heteroatoms, indicating the polycyclic-heterocyclic structure in the basal aromatic plane of N,S-GQD [22]. Further the emission characteristics (Fig. 1E) shows an excitation independent emission with a hasty diminution in the peak intensities with rise in excitation wavelengths, is a typical feature of carbon-based nano material, again confirms the N,S-GQD formation [18]. The observations mentioned above are in good agreement with the absorption and emission characteristics of GQD [24]. The images (Fig. 1Fa&b) show the emission characteristics of N,S-GQD under visible and short UV radiation of 365 nm respectively, shows that the N,S-GQD is having a blueish-white emission. In addition to solution-state luminescence, N,S-GQD exhibits intense solid-state yellow emission as well ((Fig 1Fc&d).

Further characterization studies were extended with FTIR, Raman and XRD analysis. The FTIR spectra of N,S-GQD is given in Fig S1A. The peak at ∼3401 cm−1 corresponds to the NH stretching. The additional peaks in the N,S-GQD spectrum agrees with the stretching vibrations of CH (∼ 2954 cm−1), SH (2623 cm−1), CN (2104 cm−1), amide (1654 cm−1), benzenoid ring CC (1575 cm−1), quinonoid ring CC (1408 cm−1), CN (1229 cm−1) and the CS (1159 cm−1) in consort with in-plane CN bending vibration at 1074 cm−1 [18,20]. Thus, the presence of effective N and S functionalities are confirmed through FTIR analyses. The Raman spectral analysis of N,S-GQD (Fig S1B) discloses the D and G bands at approximately 1323 cm−1 and 1520 cm−1, respectively. The ID/IG ratio of 0.87 indicates that the N,S-GQD have a relatively high quality [25]. The XRD patterns (Fig S1C) of N,S-GQD consist of sharp peaks that represents the crystallinity of the N,S-GQD. The peak around 2θ = 18.2° associated with (101) hexagonal reflections. The peak appears at 2θ = 23° corresponded to the (002) graphitic plane reflections and another peak at 2θ = 26.6° with (102) confirms graphitic plane reflections of the synthesized N,S-GQD [26].

The XPS analysis survey scan spectrum of the N,S-GQD (Fig S1D), exhibited the peaks matching to C1s, N1s, and S 2p [15,27–29]. The high-resolution spectrum of C 1 s (Fig S1E) consists of four prominent peaks at approximately 284.7 eV, 285.5 eV, 286.4 eV, and 289 eV, which were assigned to C-C/C=C, CN, CS, O-C=O groups [18,30]. The two major peaks at 398.8 eV and 399.2 eV present in the high-resolution spectrum (Fig S1F) of N 1 s were attributed to the pyridinic and pyrrolic N, respectively [20]. The O 1 s spectrum (Fig S1G) was deconvoluted into three peaks, which represents CO at 530.8 eV, CO at 531.1 eV and 531.4 eV. The high-resolution spectrum of S 2p contains peaks at 153 and 154 eV, indicating the existence of C-S-C units [31], corresponding to the thiophene functionality, indicating that the S-doping is mainly in the aromatic skeleton than at the surface functionalities. This insertion of S in the aromatic skeleton as thiophene is facilitated by the precursor MET. Thus, the formation of the aromatic graphitic structure of GQD with 6.7 % of N and 5.1 % S doping is confirmed through XPS analyses. Thus, the above characterization studies confirms the successful formation of N,S-GQD. In accordance with the information from the characterization analyses, an illustrative structure of the N,S-GQD anticipated, is shown in Fig S2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: EC sensing studies and the mechanism of HMF sensing
ce:section.ce:para: The N,S-GQD/GCE was explored for its sensing capability towards HMF using CV and DPV. The CV and DPV profiles corresponding to 1 mM HMF are shown in Fig S3. The result showed that there is ∼18-fold (Fig S3A) and ∼ 24-fold (Fig S3B) enhancement in the current responses in CV and DPV towards 1 mM HMF compared to that of the blank solution, respectively. The reduction of HMF in acidic, basic and neutral mediums are reported already [32]. To understand the influence of pH on the sensing property, HMF sensing studies were carried out in acidic, neutral and basic electrolytes. The results (Fig S4) showed that the current response become higher as the pH of the electrolytes varied from acidic to basic and the maximum current response is obtained in basic electrolyte solution. Hence, we carried out further studies in basic electrolyte solution, 0.1 M NaOH, and the reaction pathway follows the alkaline strategy. In alkaline medium, the reduction process (Fig S5) of HMF to 2,5-furandimethanol included, addition of H2O, followed by dehydroxylation [33]. The first step (i) involves the addition of an electron to HMF molecule being the slow process and there by determines the rate of the reaction. Further the reduction proceeded with the addition of another electron (ii). The negative charge of HMF is more concentrated on O atom than C of -CHO group. Thus, the addition of first H2O molecule occurs on O and then followed by C (iii). Then the molecule will undergo dehydroxylation to form 2,5-furandimethanol. This mechanism implies two mono-electronic charge transfers. This transfer is augmented in a basic medium owing to the formation of a stable carbanion. Thus, better reduction peaks are observed in basic electrolytes.

The capability of N,S-GQD to quantitatively analyses HMF is demonstrated in Fig. 2 . The CV (Fig. 2 A) and the DPV (Fig. 2B) show the variation of current responses with rise in the concentrations of HMF from 1 μM to 1 mM. The limit of detection (LOD) obtained was 10 μM in both CV (Fig. 2C) and DPV (Fig. 2D). The corresponding linear dynamic ranges (LDR) obtained for the variation of current response with concentration in DPV is shown in Fig. 2E. The LDR showed two linear fit ranges for a wider range of concentration from 10−6 to 10−3 M. The lower concentrations range from 10−6 to 10−4 M comes under one linear fit with a coefficient of correlation of R2 ≥ 0.9883 and the second linear fit corresponds to 10−4 to 10−3 M with a coefficient of correlation of R2 ≥ 0.9035. The sensitivity value (calculations in supplementary file) from the respective LDR is 0.756 μAμM−1 cm−2. In order to understand the mechanism involved in the process, scan-rate studies (Fig S6) were carried out. The scan rate values were varied from 10 to 100 mV s−1 to understand the mechanism involved in the process. The study shows that the current response vs. the scan rate is giving a linear fit of R2 = 0.9958 and the current response vs. the square root of scan rate is giving a linear fit of 0.9623. This indicates that there is not much difference in the linearity in both the cases. If the current response vs. the scan rate gives a more linear fit, it corresponds to an adsorption-controlled process, while in diffusion-controlled process the current response vs. the square root of the scan rate gives more linear fit [20]. Since the linear values obtained are not having much difference, to understand the limiting factor in the reduction process, log I versus log ν graph was plotted. From the graph, the slope obtained was 0.409. This indicates that the limiting factor in the reduction process is the diffusion process. If the slope of log I versus log ν is ∼1, it gives adsorption -controlled process and if the slope is ∼0.5, it become diffusion-controlled process [34].

As the EC sensing studies of N,S-GQD was explored in detail with GCE; the sensing studies were explored towards the EC detection characteristics of N,S-GQD/SPE to HMF by DPV. The primary DPV (Fig. 3 A) analyses were conducted by means of 1 mM HMF in 0.1 M NaOH. The DPV displays that the current response of 1 mM HMF displays a ∼ 60-fold increment in the current response as compared to the blank current. The response of current obtained for 1 mM HMF on N,S-GQD/GCE was ∼24-fold higher than that of the blank solution (Fig. 3B). On comparing these results (Fig. 3C), it can be observed that, the obtained output current on N,S-GQD/SPE is ∼2.5-fold higher than that of the response of current by N,S-GQD/GCE. The remarkable increase in the response of current for HMF on N,S-GQD modified SPE is mostly attributable to SPE's superior characteristics over GCE. The three electrodes (WE, CE, and RE) are closer together when the conventional three-electrode system is converted to SPE [35]. Faster analyte diffusion on the SPE surface is made possible by this decrease, which improves the electrocatalytic characteristics. In summary, the utilization of highly conductive carbon ink, shorter inter-electrode distances, quicker analyte diffusion, and a decreased electrolyte demand all combine to provide enhanced SPE performance [23]. Subsequent experiments focused on evaluating the LOD using N,S-GQD/SPE. The LOD determined for this electrode was 1 μM (Fig. 3D), which is significantly lower, by a factor of 10, compared to the LOD obtained on the GCE, which was 10 μM. Thus, the improved sensing features of SPE, combined with their cost-effectiveness, portability, and simplicity compared to bulkier three-electrode systems, establish SPE sensors as a highly promising entrant for real-time detection applications.

Table 1 compares the analytical performances of the N,S-GQD/SPE with the various reported EC HMF sensors based on the LOD values and the LDR. The HMF sensing studies are carried out with various sensing techniques such as CV, DPV, SWV, Chronoamperometry and LSV [32,36–43], that are included in Table 1. Karen et al. [44] reported the sensing of HMF by an electropolymerized molecularly imprinted polymer in combination with salting-out assisted liquid-liquid extraction (SALLE) with a LOD of 0.372 ppm. The procedure follows combining SALLE and an electropolymerized molecularly imprinted polymer for the detection and quantification of HMF in coffee samples. This seems to be complicated, and time-consuming procedure compared the present work, where the SPE modified N,S-GQD can detect HMF within seconds, more over the synthesis of N,S-GQD follows a simple hydrothermal strategy. The Table 1 shows that the performance of the N,S-GQD/SPE was comparable in terms of the sensor sensing property and the detection limit to that of the previous HMF sensor reports. Even though, the LOD obtained on N,S-GQD/SPE is not the lowest one, it could reach in the range of the lowest LOD values reported hitherto.

Selectivity is a significant criterion for ensuring a sensor's real-world applicability [45]. The CV (Fig S7A) and DPV curves (Fig S7B) of the 1 mM HMF in the presence of 1× concentrations of other similar carcinogenic molecules and DPV curves of individual potential interferents in the presence of HMF (1 mM each) (Fig S7C) are given in the supporting information file. From the figures, it is well clear that there is no substantial response of current for any of the other analytes except HMF, in both CV and DPV analyses demonstrating the specific sensing of HMF on N,S-GQD modified electrodes.

The specificity of N,S-GQD towards HMF can be possibly through the interaction between the heteroatom O in HMF with the heteroatoms N and S in N,S-GQD. The selective interaction between the HMF and N,S-GQD is mainly through dipole-dipole interaction between N and S in N,S-GQD and O in HMF, and also the hydrogen bonding between O in HMF and NH in N,S-GQD [46]. Further, in HMF the furan heterocyclic ring is present and since O is electronegative in nature, O draws the electronic density of the ring towards itself. Thus, the carbonyl carbon in the -CHO group present on the second position of HMF have more tendency to accept electrons. During the interaction with N,S-GQD, due to its electron rich nature, it can donate electrons to the carbonyl carbon. Further the reaction proceeds to the reduction of HMF to 2,5-furandimethanol as mentioned in section 3.2. An illustration of the EC sensing strategy of N,S-GQD/SPE towards HMF is depicted in Scheme 2 .

The reproducibility of the fabricated electrodes is essential for its practical applications [47]. The reproducibility of the electrodes was assessed by measuring the relative standard deviation (RSD) of peak currents for independently fabricated electrodes. A total of 5 electrodes were tested, and the RSD was calculated to be approximately 0.75 % (Fig S8), indicating a high degree of reproducibility. This low variation confirms the consistency in the electrode fabrication process. Further studies focused on exploring their performance in the sensing of various real samples.

Identified amounts of HMF were mixed with the real samples: honey, syrup and milk, and the calculated recovery quantities were obtained from the peak current values. The measured values were briefed in Table 2 , and corresponding differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) curves are provided as Fig S9. The electrodes demonstrated high accuracy in detecting HMF in all the tested real samples. The recovery values ranged from 99.1 % to 99.6 %, as abridged in Table 2. Thus, these results confirmed the electrodes' effectiveness in real sample analysis, further indicating their potential for food quality monitoring applications.

In this study, we successfully demonstrated a paper based screen-printed electrode (SPE) system for the detection of HMF, a potential carcinogen in foods. The SPE was modified with N,S-GQD, synthesized form amino acid precursors through simple hydrothermal process. The amino acids chosen were tryptophan and methionine, in order to facilitate effective N and S doping, respectively. The amino acid combination is not at all explored till date and are intended to the generate GQD with effective N and S doping in the aromatic skeleton rather than being the surface functionalities. We could achieve N,S-GQD as expected, and are capable of selectively sensing HMF. HMF being a carcinogenic substance present mainly in monosaccharide-rich food items, that are consumed on a regular basis, such as fruit juices, brown sugar, fruit jams, honey, milk, grain-based items, and bread, making its detection inevitable. Thus, a miniaturised printed paper based SPE system was developed for HMF detection in order to facilitate a cost-effective, portable and real-time sensing method. The LOD obtained was 1 μM with commendable sensitivity and linear range. The N,S-GQD/SPE was capable of detecting HMF in μM concentrations in real-samples as well, with creditable recovery values of ∼99 % in all the tested samples, showing its potentiality in real-time monitoring.

Aleena: Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Achu Chandran: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Conceptualization.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The supplementary data contains graphs corresponding to the characterisations FTIR, XRD and XPS along with an illustrative structure of the N,S-GQD. Also DPV graphs of futher electrochemical sensing studies are also included. The reduction reactions of the analyte and the sensitivity calculations also available.
